questions.scenario:
A health check passes on port `3000`, but `systemctl status app.service` reports the service failed. The host shows:

```text
$ ss -ltnp | grep ':3000'
LISTEN 0 511 127.0.0.1:3000 0.0.0.0:* users:(("node",pid=2601,fd=18))

$ ps -o pid,ppid,user,cmd -p 2601
  PID  PPID USER   CMD
 2601  2310 deploy node /home/deploy/app/server.js

$ ps -p 2310 -o pid,cmd
  PID CMD
 2310 -bash
```

What is the best diagnosis?

options.text: The unit likely uses socket activation, so a shell-owned Node process is expected to inherit the managed listener
The health check is reaching a stray copy started from the deploy shell, while the intended systemd service can still be failed
The failed status is stale and should be cleared because a passing health check identifies the process as the unit's MainPID
The unit probably forked into the deploy shell, so PPID `2310` still demonstrates systemd supervision

questions.explanation: PID `2601` owns the port and its PPID points to an interactive bash process, not PID 1 or the service manager. A manually started copy can keep a health check green while the managed unit is failed. A shell parent does not make a live listener a zombie. The [ps manual](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/ps.1.html) documents PID, PPID, state, and command fields.

questions.scenario:
A helper exits, but remains in the process table:

```text
$ ps -o pid,ppid,stat,cmd -p 30241
  PID  PPID STAT CMD
30241 29810 Z    [sync-helper] <defunct>
```

Sending `kill -KILL 30241` does not remove the row. What should the owner fix?

options.text: Increase the zombie's nice priority so it receives enough CPU to complete cleanup
Delete `/proc/30241` so the kernel releases the process record
Send HUP to the zombie because HUP is the only signal accepted after exit
Fix or restart parent PID `29810` so it collects the child's exit status and reaps the zombie

questions.explanation: A zombie has already finished running. Its remaining entry holds the exit status until the parent collects it, so another signal cannot kill it again. `/proc` is kernel-provided state, not a directory to delete manually, and priority cannot schedule a dead process. The [proc manual](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man5/proc.5.html) and [ps manual](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/ps.1.html) document process state.

questions.scenario: A developer starts `node server.js` over SSH and verifies the app. After the laptop disconnects, the process disappears and no restart occurs. Which production change addresses the ownership problem?

options.text: Run the long-lived app as a systemd service so PID 1 owns lifecycle, logs, boot behavior, and restart policy
Add `&` to the shell command because every background job survives its parent SSH session and reboots
Raise the app's nice value because lower CPU priority prevents terminal hangups
Store the process PID in DNS so the next SSH session can reconnect to the same process

questions.explanation: A shell background job remains tied to the interactive session and has no declared restart or boot policy. A systemd unit makes the service lifecycle explicit and independent of the operator's terminal. Nice values affect CPU scheduling, not supervision. The [systemd service manual](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/systemd.service.html) documents managed service lifecycle.

questions.scenario:
The site is slow and an operator captures:

```text
$ ps -eo pid,ppid,user,%cpu,%mem,etime,stat,cmd --sort=-%cpu | head
  PID  PPID USER  %CPU %MEM ELAPSED STAT CMD
 1842     1 app   197.0 22.1 04:02:11 Rsl  /usr/bin/node /srv/app/current/server.js
  913   912 www    12.3  1.1 14-03:12 S    nginx: worker process
```

What should happen before an immediate restart?

options.text: Assume PID `1842` is duplicated because CPU above 100 percent is impossible on Linux
Kill PID `913` because the shorter command line indicates it owns the application traffic
Capture service logs, recent deploy context, traffic, and process details for PID `1842`, then use its systemd unit for controlled action
Change the DNS record because sorted process output cannot identify local CPU pressure

questions.explanation: The sorted snapshot identifies PID `1842` as the main CPU consumer and PPID 1 shows it is supervised. CPU can exceed 100 percent when a process uses more than one core. Capturing evidence before a controlled service action preserves the clues needed for root cause. The [ps manual](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/ps.1.html) defines process selection and output fields, and the [top manual](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/top.1.html) covers live resource views.

questions.scenario: During a deploy, plain `kill 1842` starts a graceful shutdown, but the process is still present after ten seconds. Logs show it is draining active requests. Which escalation policy is safest?

options.text: Send SIGKILL immediately because plain `kill` targets only shell jobs and cannot signal a PID
Allow a bounded drain period, inspect state and logs, and use SIGKILL only if graceful SIGTERM fails and force is justified
Send SIGSTOP so the process can continue draining while execution is paused
Delete the executable from disk because a running process cannot receive signals while its file exists

questions.explanation: Plain `kill` sends SIGTERM by default, allowing the process to run cleanup handlers. A short, bounded drain protects in-flight work. SIGKILL bypasses cleanup and belongs at the end of the escalation path. SIGSTOP pauses execution, so it cannot help the process drain. The [signal manual](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/signal.7.html) documents default actions and uncatchable signals.

questions.scenario: During a data export, an operator starts `long-report-generator &` in an SSH shell and records `[1] 2409`. The shift changes before the export finishes, so a teammate opens a separate SSH session and tries `fg %1`, but that shell reports no such job. Both users are on the same host and can see PID `2409` in `ps`. Which explanation should guide the handoff?

options.text: The first shell detached the process from Linux, so the second operator must wait for the original shell to reconnect
The second operator needs the same numeric job ID, but `%1` will become available after running `jobs` once
Job number `%1` belongs to the first shell's job table, while PID `2409` is the system-wide process identity
The process entered a separate PID namespace when backgrounded, so the visible PID cannot be signaled from either shell

questions.explanation: `[1]` is a shell-local job number, while `2409` is the Linux PID visible system-wide. Another shell can inspect or signal the PID if permissions allow, but it cannot use the first shell's `%1` job-control reference. Backgrounding does not make a process supervised or immune to session loss. The [bash job-control documentation](https://www.gnu.org/software/bash/manual/html_node/Job-Control-Basics.html) describes shell job tables.

questions.scenario: A backup competes with the web app for CPU. The team wants the backup to yield more readily without stopping it. Which action and verification fit the goal?

options.text: Run or renice the backup to a higher nice value such as `10`, then verify the NI field for its PID
Set nice to `-20` as an unprivileged user because lower numbers always reduce priority
Send HUP because every process interprets HUP as a request to use less CPU
Change the backup's PPID to the Nginx worker so the scheduler favors web traffic

questions.explanation: A higher nice value lowers the process's CPU scheduling priority relative to other work. The NI field verifies the applied value. Negative nice values raise priority and normally require privilege, while signals and parent IDs do not provide a generic CPU-throttling contract. The [nice manual](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/nice.1.html) documents priority adjustment.

questions.scenario: During an outage, an investigator follows three worker processes instead of treating a visible PID as proof of health. Worker 1842 has stdout directed to `/var/log/app/stdout.log` and descriptor 18 linked to `socket:[48122]`. A second worker remains in state `D` inside an NFS wait channel after SIGKILL was requested. The kernel journal reports `Out of memory: Killed process 26631 (java)` for the third. Which interpretations belong in the incident timeline? Select all that apply.

options.text: Worker 1842's descriptor 18 should be investigated as another log file because descriptors above stderr conventionally map to disk output
Case amber shows stdout is directed to the log and the process has an open socket descriptor
Case blue can remain visible until the blocked kernel I/O returns, even after a force-kill request
The Java worker should be classified as an application-requested shutdown unless its own log contains an uncaught exception
Case green should lead to memory history, service limits, traffic, and allocation-path checks rather than an assumption of clean app exit

questions.explanation: `/proc/<pid>/fd` exposes real descriptor targets, including logs and sockets. A `D` state is uninterruptible kernel wait, so even SIGKILL may not take effect until I/O returns. An OOM-killer event is forced termination, not a graceful application shutdown, and needs memory-pressure investigation. The [proc manual](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man5/proc.5.html) and [signal manual](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/signal.7.html) cover these observations.
